mission.title: Install and Configure OpenEverest on Kubernetes
mission.description: OpenEverest is an open-source platform for automated database provisioning and management. It supports multiple database technologies (PostgreSQL, MongoDB, MySQL) and can be hosted on any Kubernetes infrastructure, in the cloud or on-premises. OpenEverest is installed via Helm and provides a web UI for managing database clusters.

steps.title: Verify prerequisites
steps.description:
Ensure you have the required tools installed and a running Kubernetes cluster:

```bash
kubectl get nodes
helm version
```

You need:
- Kubernetes cluster (EKS, GKE, or any conformant cluster)
- Helm v3
- kubectl configured with cluster access
- yq (https://github.com/mikefarah/yq)

steps.title: Add the Percona Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Percona Helm repository which hosts the OpenEverest charts:

```bash
helm repo add percona https://percona.github.io/percona-helm-charts/
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install OpenEverest using Helm
steps.description:
Install the OpenEverest core components into the everest-system namespace:

```bash
helm install everest-core percona/everest \
  --namespace everest-system \
  --create-namespace
```

Optionally set a custom admin password:

```bash
helm install everest-core percona/everest \
  --namespace everest-system \
  --create-namespace \
  --set server.initialAdminPassword=<your-password>
```

steps.title: Wait for components to be ready
steps.description:
Wait for the OpenEverest API and operator deployments to become ready:

```bash
kubectl wait --for=condition=available deployment/everest-server -n everest-system --timeout=300s
kubectl get pods -n everest-system
```

steps.title: Access the OpenEverest UI
steps.description:
Access the OpenEverest web UI using port-forward:

```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/everest 8080:8080 -n everest-system
```

Then open http://127.0.0.1:8080 in your browser.

Alternatively, expose via LoadBalancer:

```bash
helm upgrade everest-core percona/everest \
  --namespace everest-system \
  --set service.type=LoadBalancer
kubectl get svc/everest -n everest-system
```

steps.title: Retrieve the admin password
steps.description:
If you did not set a custom password during installation, retrieve the generated admin password:

```bash
kubectl get secret everest-accounts -n everest-system \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.users\.yaml}' | base64 --decode | yq '.admin.passwordHash'
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Confirm all OpenEverest components are running:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n everest-system
kubectl get crd | grep everest
helm list -n everest-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the OpenEverest release from the everest-system namespace:

```bash
helm uninstall everest-core --namespace everest-system
```

Note: If uninstallation hangs due to ImagePullBackOff on the bitnami/kubectl pre-delete hook job, manually delete the stuck job:

```bash
kubectl delete job -l app.kubernetes.io/component=pre-delete-hook -n everest-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the Custom Resource Definitions and namespace:

```bash
kubectl delete crd -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=everest
kubectl delete namespace everest-system
kubectl get namespaces | grep everest
```

upgrade.title: Update Helm repo and upgrade
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade OpenEverest:

```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade everest-core percona/everest \
  --namespace everest-system
```

Note: If upgrading from older versions, check the release notes for breaking changes. Some versions (e.g., 1.7.0, 1.8.0) had known upgrade issues with empty namespace references.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of OpenEverest pods after upgrade:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n everest-system
helm list -n everest-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Installation stuck at provisioning namespace
troubleshooting.description:
If installation hangs at 'Provisioning database namespace', check for ImagePullBackOff on the everest-operators-installer job:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n everest-system -l job-name=everest-operators-installer
kubectl describe pod -n everest-system -l job-name=everest-operators-installer
```

This is often caused by Docker Hub rate limits on the bitnami/kubectl image. Use a pull secret or mirror the image.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
Check the logs of the affected pod for errors:

```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=everest -n everest-system --tail=100
kubectl describe pod -l app.kubernetes.io/name=everest -n everest-system
```

troubleshooting.title: OIDC authentication not working
troubleshooting.description:
If using OIDC (e.g., Azure AD), verify the configuration:

```bash
kubectl get configmap everest-settings -n everest-system -o yaml
```

Ensure the issuer URL, client ID, and scopes are correct. For Azure, the issuer URL should be https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/v2.0.

troubleshooting.title: Cannot create database cluster with ClusterIP
troubleshooting.description:
If creating a database cluster fails with 'Unsupported value: ClusterIP', use 'internal' or 'external' for the proxy expose type instead:

```bash
kubectl get databasecluster -A
```

The spec.proxy.expose.type field accepts 'internal' or 'external', not 'ClusterIP'.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of OpenEverest will have the everest-server and everest-operator pods running in the everest-system namespace. The web UI is accessible via port-forward at http://127.0.0.1:8080 or via a LoadBalancer/NodePort service. You can then provision PostgreSQL, MongoDB, and MySQL database clusters through the UI.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add percona https://percona.github.io/percona-helm-charts/
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with Helm v3 and kubectl configured.
